questions.questionText: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) screening is recommended for:
questions.optionB: Men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked (one-time ultrasound screening)
questions.explanation: AAA screening: USPSTF recommends one-time abdominal USG for men age 65-75 who have ever smoked. AAA: pathologic aortic dilation >3 cm (normal infrarenal aorta ~2 cm). Most are infrarenal (95%). Risk factors: smoking (#1), age, male sex, family history, hypertension. Most asymptomatic (detected incidentally). Ruptured AAA: catastrophic triad — abdominal/back pain, hypotension, pulsatile mass (50% die before hospital). Treatment: <5.5 cm → surveillance. ≥5.5 cm or symptomatic → repair (open or EVAR). Reference: Bailey & Love — AAA.

questions.questionText: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the lower limb is best diagnosed by:
questions.optionB: Compression ultrasonography (CUS) — non-compressible vein is diagnostic
questions.explanation: DVT diagnosis: compression ultrasound (CUS) — first-line, non-invasive, >95% sensitivity for proximal DVT. Normal vein: completely compressible. DVT: non-compressible vein (echogenic thrombus may be visible). Clinical: unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, Homan's sign (unreliable). D-dimer: high NPV (negative rules out), poor specificity. Wells score: pre-test probability. Treatment: anticoagulation — LMWH/fondaparinux → warfarin/DOAC × 3-6 months. Complication: PE (most feared), post-thrombotic syndrome. Reference: Bailey & Love — DVT.

questions.questionText: Varicose veins of the lower limb are most commonly due to:
questions.optionB: Incompetence of valves in the great saphenous vein system (superficial venous incompetence)
questions.explanation: Varicose veins: dilated, tortuous superficial veins due to valvular incompetence. Great saphenous vein (GSV): most commonly affected (medial leg). Risk factors: prolonged standing, pregnancy, obesity, family history, female sex. Tests: Trendelenburg test (SFJ incompetence), Perthes test (deep vein patency), tourniquet test, duplex USG (gold standard — maps reflux). Complications: venous eczema, lipodermatosclerosis, ulceration (medial malleolar — venous ulcer), thrombophlebitis, hemorrhage. Treatment: compression, sclerotherapy, endovenous ablation (LASER/RFA), stripping. Reference: Bailey & Love — Varicose Veins.

questions.questionText: Critical limb ischemia is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Rest pain (especially at night, relieved by dependency), non-healing ulcers/gangrene, and ABI <0.4
questions.explanation: Critical limb ischemia (CLI): most severe form of peripheral arterial disease. Features: rest pain (forefoot, nocturnal, relieved by hanging leg down — gravity-assisted perfusion), non-healing ulcers (arterial — punched out, painful, distal, no bleeding base), gangrene. ABI (ankle-brachial index): <0.4 (normal: 0.9-1.3). Risk of limb loss if untreated. Treatment: revascularization (bypass surgery or angioplasty/stent), amputation if non-salvageable. Risk factors: smoking, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia. 6 P's of acute ischemia: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia. Reference: Bailey & Love — CLI.
questions.conceptTag: Critical limb ischemia rest pain ABI
